fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Flexi Cap Fund - Direct

Stability cushion near goal completion. 65% equity with debt protection. 5-year return: 18.9%. Protects against market crash in year 4-5. Part of Tranche 1 (Son's IIT/Abroad Education).

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap - Direct

allocations.rationale: Growth anchor with phenomenal 5-year return 21.7% and alpha 18.62%. Manager finds multi-year compounders. Part of Tranche 1 (Son's IIT/Abroad Education). Also core holding in Tranches 2 and 3.
Wealth creation at its best. 21.8% 5-year CAGR, alpha 14.48%, Sharpe 1.0. Experienced large-AUM (₹24k Cr) fund. Part of Tranches 2 and 3 (Daughter's Wedding and Retirement).
Diversify mid-cap exposure with proven manager. 21.7% 5-year CAGR, alpha 15.17%. Spread conviction across two skilled mid-cap pickers for better odds. Part of Tranches 2 and 3.
Inflation hedge. Gold historically delivers 8-10% real returns. Acts as non-correlated hedge for long-term horizons (10-15 years). Part of Tranches 2 and 3.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct

allocations.rationale: Stability backbone with beta 0.98 (market-like). 17.7% 5-year return. Catches broader bull market gains. Minimal drag from large-cap exposure. Part of Tranche 3 (Retirement).

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1: Assess your current position (Week 1)** - Log into your bank account. Confirm net investable corpus after home loan obligations: ₹30L (₹80L assets - ₹50L liabilities). Check if home loan is repayable early; if yes, consider one-time payment only if it improves cash flow without affecting goal targets. Otherwise, let it run its course — your 18% portfolio returns beat 7-8% home loan interest post-tax relief.

**Step 2: Open a direct mutual fund account (Week 1-2)** - Use Groww, Kuvera, or MFCentral to open a direct MF account. All are zero-cost, zero documentation, instant. Ensure you select Direct Plans (not regular) — saves 0.5-1% yearly fees vs. regular plans. Choose Growth Option (not dividend) — you want compounding, not cash dispersal.

**Step 3: Deploy lumpsum immediately (Week 2)** - Allocate ₹30L into the three tranches: Tranche 1 (son's education): ₹15L, Tranche 2 (daughter's wedding): ₹8L, Tranche 3 (retirement): ₹7L. Do not try to time the market. Markets are uncertain; time in the market beats timing the market. Start immediately. Invest lumpsum across the 7 funds in the proportions shown. This takes 30 minutes on any platform.

**Step 4: Set up automatic SIPs (Week 2)** - Create monthly SIPs on the 1st of each month. Your bank will auto-debit ₹150,000 across all funds as specified. Dates: 1st of each month, for 60 months (son's edu), 120 months (daughter), 180 months (retirement). Most platforms auto-schedule these; just set once and forget. Set calendar reminders to review quarterly.

**Step 5: Enable a portfolio dashboard (Week 3)** - Use the same platform to track your portfolio. Set up goal-wise views: one for each child, one for retirement. Check the dashboard every quarter (Jan/Apr/Jul/Oct). Spending 10 minutes every 3 months is all you need.
review_checkpoints: Every Quarter (Jan/Apr/Jul/Oct, 15 minutes each): Confirm SIPs are active. Check that ₹150k debit appears on the 1st of the month. If any SIP misses, immediately restart it on the platform. Check for scheme changes. Has any fund merged, changed managers, or closed? (Very rare, but check.) If yes, flag for deeper review. No action needed if all SIPs running, no fund changes, portfolio value is what you'd expect.

Every 6 Months (July & January, 30 minutes): Compare 1-year performance. Each fund should return within 3-5% of its 5-year average CAGR. If any fund lags by >10% for 6 months, it's not alarming yet. Check yearly fees. Direct plans should charge 1.0-1.15% expense ratio. If you notice >1.2%, you may have accidentally invested in a regular plan — switch to direct.

Every 12 Months (January, 45 minutes): 1-year returns review. Each fund's 1-year return should reflect market conditions. Large-cap indices returned ~12% in 2024? Expect Motilal Oswal to return 18-22% due to alpha. That's normal and good. Fund underperformance check. Has any fund lagged its category benchmark for 2 consecutive quarters? If yes, consider a replacement. Tax planning. In your 30% bracket, are you using LTCG optimally? (You are — all funds here are equity-taxed. Good.)

At 2.5-year mark (Mid-2027): Son's education goal update. You'll have invested ₹30L + growth. Check: Are you on track for ₹50L? If ahead, consider stopping SIPs early or shifting to a debt fund. If behind, increase SIP by 10%.

At 4.5-year mark (Mid-2029): Son's education finalization. Goal matures in 6 months. Shift from Flexi Cap and Equity & Debt into a liquid/money-market fund (0.5-2% returns, but stable). You'll draw down ₹50L over the next 1-2 years for education expenses.

At 5-year mark (Mid-2030): Daughter's wedding goal enters final phase. 5 years remain. Start reducing equity proportion. Move 30% of her tranche into an Aggressive Hybrid or Conservative Hybrid (less volatile). Keep 70% in equities — still time for growth.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
**When to rebalance:** Once a year, in January. Takes 20 minutes.

**How to rebalance:** Check your portfolio value in each tranche. Has one category (e.g., mid-cap) grown to >30% of the tranche while others stayed flat? That's drift. Drift check example: Tranche 1 allocated 40% to BOI Flexi Cap, 35% ICICI Equity & Debt, 25% Motilal Large & Mid Cap. After 1 year, if mid-cap have grown faster, the split might be 35% / 30% / 35%. That's acceptable (within 5% tolerance). If drift is >10%, sell the outperformer, buy the underperformer to return to target weights.

**Tax-efficient rebalancing:** If a fund has gained >₹1.25L, the first ₹1.25L of gains is tax-free (LTCG exemption). Sell in tranches to optimize tax. Your platform will handle this, but be aware.

**When to EXIT a fund:** If a fund underperforms its category benchmark for 2 consecutive quarters (6 months), investigate. If the underperformance is because the manager left or the fund pivoted strategy, exit immediately. Don't hope it recovers. If underperformance is just cyclical (market cap rotation), hold. Equity markets rotate; don't panic-sell.

**Replacement protocol:** Exit underperformer into a new fund in the same category with better recent performance. Example: If Edelweiss Mid Cap lags, replace with Nippon India Growth Mid Cap (both mid-cap, but fresher alpha).
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth. Start with ₹150k/month; increase to ₹165k in year 2, ₹181.5k in year 3, etc. This inflation-adjusts your contributions and accelerates goal achievement.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Initial deployment of ₹30L lumpsum across three tranches
Mid-point review; gold position increases across tranches
Son's education goal (Tranche 1) matures; shift to debt/liquid funds for drawdown
Daughter's wedding goal (Tranche 2) enters final phase; reduce equity, increase stability
Retirement tranche (Tranche 3) de-risking begins; prepare for drawdown in 2 years
Retirement goal (Tranche 3) matures; shift to income-generating bonds and gold for post-55 income

aps.reasoning: passive_instrument_fraction: All 5 pure equity funds (BOI Flexi Cap 20%, Motilal Large & Mid Cap 12.5%, HDFC Mid Cap 10%, Edelweiss Mid Cap 7.5%, Nippon Large Cap 2.5%) are actively managed — no index funds or equity ETFs in the equity sleeve. HDFC Gold ETF is passive but is excluded as it is a gold/commodity instrument, not equity. Score = 0.0. turnover_score: The plan calls for annual rebalancing (January, 20 minutes) with a 10% drift tolerance, which is relatively infrequent. However, there are quarterly SIP checks and an active fund-replacement protocol triggered after 2 consecutive quarters of underperformance, which adds some tactical activity. Overall moderately passive on turnover. Score ≈ 0.55. cost_emphasis_score: The plan does mention direct plans vs regular (saves 0.5–1%), checks expense ratios semi-annually, and flags >1.2% as a warning. However, cost is clearly secondary — the dominant selection criterion is alpha, 5-year CAGR, and manager quality. Score ≈ 0.3. research_vs_cost_score: The plan is heavily research-driven — every fund is justified by specific alpha figures (18.3%, 18.62%, 14.48%, 15.17%), fund manager narratives ('finds multi-year compounders'), AUM size, and category-specific expertise. This is the opposite of broad market passive exposure. Score ≈ 0.1. time_horizon_alignment_score: Goals span 5–15 years with long SIP tenures (60/120/180 months), 'set and forget' SIP philosophy, and structured glide-path de-risking. Quarterly reviews are light-touch. Offset slightly by active replacement triggers and tactical shifts at mid-goal milestones. Score ≈ 0.65. portfolio_activeness_score: All equity funds carry very high alpha metrics (14–18%+) against Nifty 50, active mandates (flexi cap, mid cap, large & mid cap with stock-picking mandates), and no index/ETF equity exposure. Nippon Large Cap has beta ≈ 0.98 (somewhat index-like) but no alpha data; it is still an actively managed fund. Gold ETF is passive but non-equity. ICICI Equity & Debt is an actively managed hybrid. The portfolio is overwhelmingly active in character. Score ≈ 0.07.

pqs.reasoning: GOAL ALIGNMENT (0.52): The three-tranche structure mapping to each goal (son's education 5yr/50L, daughter's wedding 10yr/30L, retirement 15yr/3Cr) is conceptually sound. However, the projected corpus numbers are wildly inconsistent with the stated inputs — a 5yr base-case corpus of ₹15L when the plan deploys ₹18.5L lumpsum plus ~₹97K/month SIPs at 16% CAGR should yield ~₹130L, not ₹15L. The 15yr retirement base-case of ₹85L is also far short of the 3Cr target. The son's 5yr education tranche is 100% equity at inception, which is aggressive for a moderate investor with a short near-term goal and no debt cushion. The SIP step-up of 10% is well-aligned with the investor's senior professional life stage. DIVERSIFICATION (0.52): Seven funds across flexi-cap, aggressive hybrid, large & mid-cap, mid-cap (two funds), gold ETF, and large-cap provide reasonable breadth and multi-AMC spread. However, mid-cap weight is elevated (17.5% combined) for a self-described moderate investor, there is zero international exposure, and no pure debt allocation despite near-term goals. Gold at 5% is too small to provide meaningful diversification benefit. RISK-RETURN APPROPRIATENESS (0.38): The plan is effectively 100% equity at inception across all three tranches including the 5-year education goal — inappropriate for a moderate investor with a near-term liquidity need. The stated 18.5% 15yr base-case CAGR is optimistic. The gold rationale incorrectly states '8–10% real returns' when gold delivers nominal returns in that range at best. No downside scenario planning for the 5yr tranche and no debt buffer. The allocation schedule does show sensible de-risking over time per tranche, which partially mitigates concerns. INTERNAL CONSISTENCY (0.22): Multiple serious contradictions undermine the plan. (1) Monthly SIPs total only ₹97K (30+25+20+12+5+3.5+1.5) versus the investor's stated ₹1.5L/month capacity — ₹53K unaccounted. (2) Lumpsum total across funds is ₹18.5L but setup phase instructs deploying ₹30L. (3) Projected corpus figures are mathematically inconsistent with allocation amounts and stated CAGR assumptions by an order of magnitude. (4) Rebalancing guidelines cite Tranche 1 weights as '40% BOI Flexi Cap, 35% ICICI, 25% Motilal' but actual allocations are 20%, 17.5%, 12.5%. (5) Rationale and risks fields are empty. (6) Allocation schedule shows 0% gold at year 0 but the gold ETF SIP starts immediately. TAX EFFICIENCY (0.45): The plan correctly uses equity-taxed wrappers throughout (all funds qualify for LTCG 12.5% treatment), mentions the ₹1.25L LTCG exemption in rebalancing, and recommends Growth option over dividend — all positive for a 30% slab investor. However, the plan omits ELSS entirely despite the investor being in the 30% old-regime slab where ₹1.5L 80C headroom could save ~₹46K annually in taxes. The year-5 drawdown plan recommends shifting to 'liquid/money-market funds' which would be taxed at slab (30%), whereas arbitrage funds or short-duration equity-oriented funds would deliver equity taxation at similar risk — a meaningful missed opportunity the plan does not address.
